name: Space-Based Architecture

forces: Removing the database bottleneck ↔ replication / consistency cost
x.structure: Processing units hold data in a replicated in-memory grid ("space"); a middleware tier handles replication, messaging, and data partitioning, removing the central database from the request path.
antiPatterns: Treating the grid as a cache afterthought (no durability plan)
x.scale: High-throughput application; trading, ticketing, real-time bidding, gaming.

def: A high-scalability architecture that keeps working data in a replicated in-memory grid across processing units, eliminating the central database as a bottleneck under extreme, variable load.
problem: A shared relational database becomes the throughput and elasticity bottleneck for very high, spiky concurrent load.
mechanism: Stateless-ish processing units read/write data in a distributed in-memory "space" (tuple space / data grid). Middleware replicates and partitions data, routes messages, and asynchronously persists to durable storage off the hot path, so the system scales by adding units.
limits: Risk of data loss / split-brain on failures
whenNot: Modest / steady load (a database is simpler)
Strong durability and ACID are paramount
myths: "It’s just caching" — the data grid is the system of record on the hot path, not a side cache
